segments.s711-g1: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed at the start for emphasis. داستانهای دیرینه is a noun phrase 'stories/tales (داستانهای)' followed by the adjective 'دیرینه' ('ancient') in postnominal order (classical/poetic style). گوی is an imperative or hortative verb 'tell/speak' addressed to a teller; in classical Persian poetry this short form directs the singer/narrator to recite the named tales.
segments.s711-g2: سخنهای بهرام چوبینه is a genitive/attributive sequence meaning 'the words/poems of Bahram Chubin' (سخنهای = 'words/poems', بهرام = name, چوبینه an epithet 'of the club' describing Bahram). The final گوی repeats the imperative 'speak' and links this clause to the previous one as a second command.
segments.s711-g3: که چون functions as a subordinating conjunction 'when/after' (classical usage). او is the subject 'he', سوی شهر ترکان is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward the city of the Turks' (شهر modified by ترکان), and رسید is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s711-g4: به نزد is a compound prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of'. دلیر و بزرگان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the brave and the great (nobles)'. رسید is again past 3rd-singular 'arrived/came' and completes the clause; the repetition of رسید is a poetic parallelism.
segments.s711-g5: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گردان بیدار دل is a noun phrase 'a troop/band (گردان) of wakeful/alert hearts' where بیدار دل is a compound modifier meaning 'alert-hearted'. ده هزار is the numeral 'ten thousand' specifying the number taken from that troop.
segments.s711-g6: پذیره here functions as a verb/root meaning 'received/welcomed' and is combined with شدندش — شد��ند (they became/did) plus the enclitic pronoun ش (= 'him/it') — a classical periphrastic way to indicate 'they received him'. گزیده سوار is a nominal phrase 'chosen/elite riders' that characterizes those who received him.
segments.s711-g7: پسر is the nominal subject 'the son' and با برادرش is a companion phrase 'with his brother' (با + برادر + ش, the enclitic 'his'). پیش اندرون is a spatial phrase 'before/into the inner quarters' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', اندرون = 'inner area/inner court'). Word order is typical classical Persian (subject + adjuncts + locative).
segments.s711-g8: ابا here should be read as the fused conjunction + preposition 'و با' = 'and with' (a common poetic contraction); هر یکی 'each one' follows and موبدی رهنمون is 'a mobad (Zoroastrian priest) as guide' (موبدی = priest, رهنمون = guide). The whole phrase means 'and with each one a mobad as guide.'
segments.s711-g33: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' (ب + ضمیر) marking the indirect object; گفت is the simple past 3rd-person singular of گفتن 'to say' (he said); خاقان is the speaker's title 'the khagan'; که‌ای is a vocative interjection (که + ای) addressing someone as 'O…'; سرفراز is an epithet 'exalted / honorable' used as the addressee's vocative. The word order is V‑complement then title then direct address: 'He said to him, “O exalted one…”'.
segments.s711-g34: بدین روز is an adverbial phrase = به + این روز 'on this day' (ب functions as the prepositional prefix 'به' here); هرگز is the negative adverb 'never'; مبادت is a jussive/optative negative from مباد (literary) meaning 'may it not be / may there not be' (a wish/curse formula); نیاز is the noun 'need' or 'privation' (the thing being wished away). Together the clause expresses a wish: 'May there never be need on this day.'
segments.s711-g35: بدارم is a present/jussive form of داشتن here meaning 'I will have / I will hold' or 'I shall consider/keep'; تو را is the direct object 'you' with the object marker را; همچو is a comparative particle 'like/as'; پیوند خویش is a genitival phrase 'a bond of my own' (پیوند = bond/link, خویش = my own, realized as خوی + ش). Word order is verb + object + simile + nominal phrase.
segments.s711-g36: چه is an exclamatory 'what / how' introducing a rhetorical question; پیوند 'bond' is the head noun; برتر is the comparative 'higher / superior'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; فرزند خویش 'one's own child' (فرزند = child; خویش = one's own). The phrase asks rhetorically 'what bond is better than that of one's own child?'.
segments.s711-g37: همه بوم literally 'all (of the) land/people' — here 'all the inhabitants' or 'everyone in the realm'; با من 'with me' (با = with, من = me); بدین یاورند is 'are thus supporters/allies' (ب + دین is the literary form of به این/بدین 'thus/on this', یاورند = 'they are helpers/supporters'). The clause states a general present-tense fact: 'All the land stand with me as such allies.'
segments.s711-g38: اگر ... اگر is a paired concessive 'if/whether ... if/whether' introducing two alternatives; کهترانند is a predicate adjective-form meaning 'they are lesser / inferior'; مهترند is the opposite 'they are greater / senior'. The pair means 'whether they be lesser or greater' (i.e., of lower or higher rank).
segments.s711-g39: تو را is the direct object 'you' with the object marker را; بر سران is a prepositional phrase 'over/upon the chiefs/heads' (بر = upon, سران = leaders/chiefs); سرفرازی is an abstract noun 'honor / exaltation'. The verb دهم ('I give') is implied by the clause structure (or follows in the next half); together: 'I will grant you honor among the chiefs.'
segments.s711-g40: هم 'also'; از + مهتران 'from the great/elders' (از = from/of; مهتران = the greater/important ones); بی‌نیازی 'freedom from need / self-sufficiency' (an abstract noun). The verb دهم ('I give') continues here, so the line promises both honor and independence: 'I will also give you freedom from need among the great.'
segments.s711-g17: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning "when"; it introduces a temporal clause. Word order is the poetic/older Persian pattern: conjunction + subject (بهرام) + adverbial phrase (برتخت سیمین, literally "on-the-throne silver") + verb (نشست) in simple past meaning "sat." برتخت is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + تخت) meaning "on the throne."
segments.s711-g18: گرفت is a simple past transitive verb "took/seized." آن زمان is a temporal adverbial "at that time/then." The phrase دست خاقان بدست is an object phrase meaning "the khagan's hand (into) [his] hand" with بدست functioning as an adverbial/locative "in the hand / into his hand." The verb appears before the temporal phrase here because of poetic word order.
segments.s711-g19: بدو is the prepositional-advective particle here meaning "to him / unto him," so بدو گفت = "he said to him." گفت is the past-tense verb "said" (note: the lemma senses list is phrased oddly, but the form is the simple past). کای is a vocative particle "O," followed by مهتر بافرین, two epithets in apposition meaning "chief, praiseworthy." There is no copula—this is direct address.
segments.s711-g20: This is a string of titles in apposition: سپهدار (commander) + ترکان (of the Turks) linked by و to سالار (lord/commander) + چین (China). The nouns are juxtaposed without a verb; ترکان and چین are genitive/associative complements naming the domains.
segments.s711-g21: تو دانی is present-tense second-person "you know." که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. از شهریار جهان is a prepositional phrase meaning "from/of the sovereign of the world" (شهریار = king/sovereign, جهان = the world).
segments.s711-g22: نباشد is the classical negative-existential/optative form (negative of باشد) meaning here "there is not" / "is not" (no one is). کسی following the negative takes the sense "no one." ایمن اندر نهان is a small predicate phrase "safe in hiding/secretly safe." The clause literally: "no one is safe in hiding [from the sovereign]."
segments.s711-g23: بر آساید is a verbal phrase where بر + verb conveys a state—آساید (from آسودن) here has an optative/present sense "be at rest / be at ease." از گنج is the noun phrase "from/over treasure" (object or domain), and the enclitic in بگزایدش (بـ + گزاید + -ش) marks a direct object "—it," so بگزایدش = "he chooses it" (i.e., he selects/esteemes the treasure). The prefix ب- is the classical present/future marker; word order reflects poetic compression.
segments.s711-g24: تن آسان کند is a causative construction: کند (makes/does) with تن آسان = "(makes) the body easy/comfortable." رنج بفزایدش contains the verb بفزاید (adds/increases) with enclitic -ش referring back to the body (تن) and رنج as its object complement; literally "he increases its suffering." The verbs use the classical b- prefix (بفزاید) and simple کند (present/past-aspectual depending on context) typical of Shahnameh style.
segments.s711-g9: چو (when) + past verb آمد forms a temporal subordinate clause 'when he came'. بر تخت خاقان is a prepositional phrase 'onto the khaghan's throne' with the preposition بر before تخت; خاقان is a title in a genitive/attributive relation to تخت. فراز is an adverbial/locative adjunct 'on high' placed after the noun phrase to emphasize elevation.
segments.s711-g10: آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase in the simple past ('he made/paid acclamation' = 'he praised'). The initial برو here functions in older/poetic usage as a prepositional/locutional element (contextually 'upon him / to him'); کرد is the lexical past auxiliary. و links the two coordinated actions; نماز is the direct object of برد(ن) in 'بردش نماز' meaning 'he took/led him to prayer' (بردش = برد + -ش object clitic).
segments.s711-g11: چو + past دید again marks a temporal clause 'when the khaghan saw him' (ورا = 'him' as the object). برپای جست is a compact idiom: برپای ('to the feet' / 'to a standing posture') + جست (past of پرید/جهیدن) = 'sprang to his feet'. Word order is Subject (خاقان) → object clitic (ورا) → verb (دید), then the resultant action phrase.
segments.s711-g12: ببوسید is the simple past 'he kissed (him)'; و coordinates the next verb. بسترد is past of ستردن meaning 'he covered/veiled'; it takes روی + possessive ش 'his face' as its object. بدست here indicates the instrument/means 'with the hand' (i.e. 'covered his face with his hand').
segments.s711-g13: بپرسید is past 'he asked/questioned'. بسیارش is 'much/many things of him' (i.e. 'he asked him many questions / asked him at length'); از introduces the topic 'about/concerning' (از رنج راه = 'about the hardship(s) of the road'). The noun phrase رنج راه is a genitive-like compound 'hardship of the road/journey'.
segments.s711-g14: ز is the poetic variant of از 'about/from'. کار here means 'affair/doing' (the king's affairs), followed by a coordinated phrase ز پیکار 'about the battle(s)'. شاه و سپاه are coordinated nouns 'the king and the army' that further specify the subject matter being asked about. The repeated ز emphasizes separate topics.
segments.s711-g15: هم 'also' adds these additional people to those questioned. ایزد here is used as a proper/name or divine figure ('Izad'/'God' depending on context); گشسپ is a personal name (Goshasp). و links to یلان 'heroes', and سینه here qualifies them 'of the breast' = 'brave-hearted' or 'bold ones'. را is the object marker applying to the whole coordinated list.
segments.s711-g16: بپرسید again is past 'he asked/questioned', و links the clause to the prior list. خراد and برزینه here function as additional objects (likely names) and را marks them as definite objects; because the lexical senses provided are ambiguous, these two items are best read as proper names in this passage (i.e. he asked Khirad and Barzine).
segments.s711-g73: بهر functions as a preposition/distributive marker 'for/for each'; بامدادی is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at dawn' (here 'each dawn'); بهنگام + بار forms a temporal phrase 'at the time of the بار' — بار is used in the sense 'occasion/time' (poetic use). Together this whole segment sets the recurring time when the action happens.
segments.s711-g74: چنین مرد = 'such a man' (چنین qualifies مرد). دینار خواهد هزار shows a poetically inverted order: خواهد (future auxiliary/verb 'will have/receive') governs the nominal phrase 'دینار هزار' — i.e. 'will receive a thousand dinars.' The verb앞 of the numeral is a classical/poetic word order for emphasis.
segments.s711-g75: ببخشش is a nominalized/possessive-form of بخشیدن — 'his giving' or 'his gift/grant.' گرین is an adjective 'tearful' and بیستگانی here denotes a group 'of twenty' (literally 'twenty‑group'). بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause therefore states what his giving consisted of: 'his gift was twenty tearful ones.'
segments.s711-g76: همه is the subject 'all/everything'; بهر او is a beneficiary phrase 'for him'; زرکانی is the predicate noun (here 'gold‑mines/treasures') and بود is the past copula 'were/belonged.' Word order is Subject – (beneficiary) – Predicate – copula, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s711-g77: بدو 'to him' is an indirect‑object adverbial; گفت is the past verb 'said' (here reported speech); خاقان is the subject 'the khagan' placed after the verb (common in classical word order). که introduces the content clause whose topic begins with آیین ما 'our custom.'
segments.s711-g78: چنین است is a formulaic predication 'is thus/so.' و simply joins the next phrase. افروزش دین ما is a noun phrase 'the radiance/kindling (افروزش) of our religion/faith' with افروزش as head and دین ما as its genitive; the whole supplies the second linked predication.
segments.s711-g79: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/for'; از ما 'of/from us' marks the group; هر آنکس که is the indefinite relative 'whoever'; جنگی ترست is a comparative predicate: جنگی 'warlike' modified by the comparative-ending implicit in ترست (تر + است) meaning 'is more warlike.'
segments.s711-g80: به هنگام سختی is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at the time/when hardship comes.' درنگی ترست again uses درنگی (hesitation/delay, here verbal/adjectival) with ترست as the comparative construction 'is more prone to delay/hesitate.' The clause states that the more warlike man will, paradoxically, be more prone to hesitate in hardship.
segments.s711-g25: گر begins a conditional clause 'if'. ایدون is an archaic/poetic verb/adverb (literally 'come/appear') used here as a temporal/attitudinal particle 'now/if you come (to)'; که introduces the subordinate content clause. اندر is the preposition 'in/here', followed by پذیری (2nd‑person present/subjunctive form 'you accept/receive') and the object مرا (split as م + را) marking 'me' with the postposed object marker را.
segments.s711-g26: بهرنیک is an adverbial phrase 'for good' ('in good fortune'); و links the two polar circumstances 'and', followed by بد 'bad/ill'. دست‌گیری is a deverbal noun meaning 'help/assistance' and takes the object مرا (again split m + را) 'me' — so the phrase means 'give me help in good and bad'.
segments.s711-g27: بدین is an archaic locative 'in/at this' (here written as the prefix b- + دین); مرز 'border' is the place, modified by بی‌یار 'without a friend'. یار توام is a predicative phrase: یار 'companion' + توام (تو + ام) = 'I am yours' / 'I am your...', so the whole says 'on this border, friendless, I am your companion'.
segments.s711-g28: بهر نیک و بد is a formulaic adverbial 'for good and for ill' (بهر = 'for/toward' + نیک/بد). غمگسار is an agent noun 'consoler/comforter' and توام (as above) functions as 'I am (for) you' — together 'I am your consoler in good and bad'.
segments.s711-g29: وگر is another conditional 'and if'. هیچ before a positive noun here works like English 'any' (used poetically). رنج آیدت = 'suffering comes to you' (آیدت = 'it comes to you' / 'befalls you'), and بگذرم is first‑person present/subjunctive used as a future: 'I will let it pass / I will endure/forgive it.'
segments.s711-g30: زمین را سراسر is 'the whole land/earth' (را marks the definite object). بپی is a small archaic/contracted particle used here for rhythm/emphasis, and بسپرم is the first‑person verb 'I will entrust / hand over' with زمین را as its object — 'I will entrust the whole land.'
segments.s711-g31: گر ایدون که is the conditional 'if now that' (ایدون again an archaic temporal/attitudinal word). باشی is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive 'you be/are', تو is the emphatic pronoun 'you', and همداستان is the predicate adjective 'of one mind / agreeable' — 'if you are of the same mind'.
segments.s711-g32: از ایدر shوم = 'I will depart from here': از = 'from', ایدر = 'here', and شوم is 1st‑person subjunctive (classical usage for 'I shall/may go' or 'I will become [depart]'). تا به هندوستان is a range phrase 'as far as / to India' (تا = 'as far as', به = 'to').
segments.s711-g89: چو ... باشد is a classical conditional/time clause: چو = "when" and باشد here functions as the present/optative copula introducing the state ('is/being'). جهاندار بیدار = 'the sovereign (king) is alert' (adjective follows noun). The final و گرد is left hanging to link into the next segment (گرد begins the noun phrase گرد عنان 'the reins' which continues the thought).
segments.s711-g90: This completes the object phrase and main clause: عنان را = 'the reins' (object marked by را), به کهتر = 'to an inferior' (به = 'to/for'), نباید سپرد is an impersonal negative modal: نباید = 'should not/ought not to' and سپرد = 'to entrust/hand over'. The verb (سپرد) is final, so the clause reads 'one should not entrust the reins to an inferior.'
segments.s711-g91: اگر ... introduces a conditional: اگر = 'if'. ز + و here functions as the poetic prepositional element 'از/زو' meaning 'from' (the segmentation shows it as ز and the conjunction-like و), and رهانم is an archaic first‑person verb 'I free/release' (present/subjunctive flavor). تو را is the dative/affected 'for you', and شایدت is a modal clitic meaning 'it may be possible for you' (literally 'it is possible to you').
segments.s711-g92: وگر = 'but if' (introducing the alternative). ویژه here is an adjective 'special/particular' that modifies آزرم; آزرم is an obscure/possibly technical or proper term in this line (translated here as 'special favour/thing'), او = 'his', and بایدت 'is necessary for you' (impersonal/modal necessity directed at 'you'). The clause gives the counter‑case: 'but if not, his special thing/favour will be necessary to you.'
segments.s711-g93: بدو گفت خاقان is a classical word order: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object), گفت (here a past verb 'said' — the supplied sense list is nominal but the function is verbal) and خاقان is the subject 'the khagan' placed after the verb (inversion common in epic diction). که introduces the reported content clause. فرمان تو راست = 'your command is correct' (possessive following noun).
segments.s711-g94: بدین = ب + دین here functions as the fused prepositional/ demonstrative phrase 'by this' (ب- as prefix + دین); آرزو = 'wish/desire' and رای و پیمان تو = 'your counsel/opinion and your covenant/pledge' (رای = plan/counsel, پیمان = pledge/covenant). راست at the end predicates both رای و پیمان: 'your counsel and your pledge are right.'
segments.s711-g95: مرا‌گر here is a conditional frame ('مرا گر' = 'if to me' or loosely 'if for my part / if it lay with me'); توانی is second‑person present/subjunctive 'you can / you could', and رهانید is an older verbal form meaning 'you release/you freed' (here used in the conditional sense 'could free'). ازوی = 'from him' (the preposition + pronoun). Together: 'If you could free him from (me/from him/etc.).'
segments.s711-g96: سرآورده باشی is a resultative/modal cluster: سرآورده = 'that which would have come about / brought to a head', باشی = an older subjunctive/optative form meaning roughly 'you would be/have' (here rendering a counterfactual result). همه گفت و گوی = 'all talk / all discourse' (گفت و گوی is the conventional phrase 'conversation, dispute'). The whole segment gives the predicted result: 'you would have ended/brung to an end all talk.'
segments.s711-g41: بدین نیز is an adverbial phrase ('by/with this also' or 'thereupon as well') placed before the clause. بهرام is the subject. سوگند is the object/noun 'an oath'. خواست is the simple past 3rd-person singular of خواستن used here as 'took/asked' (he made/swore an oath). Word order is adverbial → subject → object → verb, which is idiomatic in poetic Persian.
segments.s711-g42: This clause expresses a wish/curse that follows the oath. زیان ('harm/detriment') is the noun subject; بود is the past copula 'was' used here in a jussive/optative sense within the oath; بر جان او literally 'upon his life' (preposition بر + noun جان + pronoun او). بند is 'fetter/bond' (the thing to be imposed) and خواست is again past 'wished/asked' — together meaning 'he wished (that) harm be a fetter upon his life' (i.e. may harm be bound to his life). The clause uses a verbless/nominal predicate feel where بود + (noun) + object + خواست yields the optative sense.
segments.s711-g43: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = said). خاقان is the subject (the khagan). به برتر خدای is a vocative/exclamatory opening ('O God Most High' / 'O exalted God') addressing God at the start of the reported speech. Note the verb گفت appearing before the subject (an inversion common in poetry) and the use of به to mark the addressee/vocative.
segments.s711-g44: که introduces the content of the speech ('that' / 'for'), هست is the copula 'is'. او is the pronoun 'he'. مرا و تو را are the two objects marked by the object marker را but here functioning like English datives 'for me and for you' (mara = 'to me', to ra = 'to you'). رهنمای is the predicate noun 'guide'. The clause literally reads 'that he is (a) guide to me and to you.' Word order subject (او) before copula is slightly emphatic in poetry, and the two datives are coordinated with و.
segments.s711-g45: که تا زنده‌ام begins a durative subordinate clause: که + تا + زنده‌ام = 'for/as long as I live'. زنده‌ام is the present-tense nominal phrase 'I am alive' (زنده + -ام). ویژه یار توام is the main predicate: ویژه (adjective 'special/dedicated') modifies یار ('friend, companion'), and توام is the 1st-person predicate 'I am (your)'. Together 'I am your special friend' — the speaker promises ongoing companionship while alive. The construction uses تا for temporal extent and places the temporal clause before the promise.
segments.s711-g46: بهر نیک و بد is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'for good and bad' (بهر = 'for/as to'; نیک و بد = 'good and bad', i.e. good times and bad times). غمگسار توام is the predicate: غمگسار ('consoler/companion in sorrow') followed by توام ('I am' / 'I will be for you'), so the whole clause says 'I will be your consoler in good and bad.' The adjective-noun + predicate-am combination gives the promise of steadfast support.
segments.s711-g47: ازان پس is an adverbial temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = from that/thereupon; پس = after/then). دو ایوان is the direct object 'two iwans/halls'. بیاراستند is the past 3rd-person plural verb 'they adorned/they decorated'. Word order places the temporal phrase first, then the object, then the verb — typical poetic ordering where the actor ('they') is not overt because the verb's ending carries the subject.
segments.s711-g48: زهر گونه‌ای جامه‌ها خواستند = 'they asked for garments of every kind.' زهر here is an archaic contraction (ẕ + har / zِ هر) meaning 'of every' or 'from every' (poetic/old-fashioned), گونه‌ای = 'kind/sort', جامه‌ها = plural 'garments', and خواستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they requested/asked for'. The phrase thus means they ordered/requested clothes in every variety.
segments.s711-g81: چو here introduces a conditional ('if/when'); خواهد is the present/future sense 'wants/desires' (3rd person); فزونی is the thing desired ('increase/surplus'); نداریم is the present negative 'we do not have'; باز is an adverb 'again/left' (here 'anymore/left'). Word order is typical classical Persian: conditional marker + verb + object + negated possessive clause.
segments.s711-g82: ز + مردان = 'from/of the men'; رزم‌آور is a compound adjective/noun 'warlike / warrior'; جنگ ساز is a compound in which ساز functions as 'maker/means' (literary usage), so 'جنگ‌ساز' = 'war‑maker' or 'instrument/creator of war'. The phrase names the kind of men being spoken of.
segments.s711-g83: This is an archaic/poetic word order: فزونی (surplus) + مر ('for me' / 'my') + او ('his') — together indicating 'his surplus is for me' — then راست ('right/true') as a predicative adjective, برما ('to/upon us') and کنون ('now') time adverb. In classical Persian, possessive/dative particles like مر can appear before the pronoun (مر او) to mean 'to/for me of him' (= 'his surplus is mine').
segments.s711-g84: بدینار is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'for a dinar' (ب + دینار). خوانیم is 1st person plural present/subjunctive form 'we recite/perform' (classical usage can mean 'we shall/let us recite'); بر وی is the prepositional object 'upon him'; فسون is the noun 'enchantment/charm' as direct object of خوانیم. The verb governs the following prepositional phrase.
segments.s711-g85: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. ز + (او) = 'from him' (contracted in the line); بازگیرم is 1st person singular present/subjunctive (or present narrative) of بازگیر 'I take back / reclaim' ('-م' marks 1sg). بجوشد is a present/subjunctive/optative 3rd person 'will seethe/boil/erupt' and سپاه is the subject/agent 'the army/host' that will 'boil' (metaphorically: rise up). The clause order places the condition first, then the result.
segments.s711-g86: ز لشکر = 'because of / from the host/army' (ز here gives cause); شود is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive of شدن 'become'; روز روشن is a noun + adjective 'bright day' (adjective follows the noun in Persian poetry at times); سیاه is predicative adjective 'black/dark'. The clause says the bright day will become dark on account of the army.
segments.s711-g87: جهانجوی is a vocative/agent 'the world‑seeker / ambitious man' (title/epithet). گفت here functions as a speech marker (classical past 'said'); ای is the vocative particle 'O'; سر انجمن = 'head/chief of the assembly' (سر = 'chief/head' used as a title, انجمن = 'assembly/council'). Together they introduce a quoted address.
segments.s711-g88: تو is the 2nd person singular 'you'; کردی is the simple past 'you did/made' (here transitive 'you caused'); و functions as a connective 'and/so' (linking clauses) in the printed segmentation but is part of the object construction in the original line; را is the definite/object marker attached to the following pronoun/phrase (here marking the person affected); خیره بر خویشتن is a participial/adjectival phrase 'staring/astonished at yourself' (خیره = 'gaping/astonished', بر خویشتن = 'at yourself'). Syntactically: 'you made him (ورا) astonished at you.'
segments.s711-g49: A simple noun list joined by و (and). Each item stands as a nominal element: پرستنده (an attendant/one who serves), پوشش (a covering, cloak), خوردنی (a substantive adjective 'things to be eaten' = food). Word order is the basic Noun [و Noun] coordination with no overt verb in this segment.
segments.s711-g50: ز (= 'from, of') introduces the following noun phrase. چیزی here functions as an indefinite pronoun 'anything/whatever'. که begins a relative clause modifying چیزی. بایست is a modal/obligative (literally 'it ought to / must') and گستردنی is a participial/adjectival form 'that is to be spread' (the -نی suffix makes it a deverbal adjective). Together the clause means 'whatever (thing) ought to be spread out.'
segments.s711-g51: ز introduces a list of materials ('of silver and golden [things]'). زرین here is an adjective/noun 'golden' following (or coordinating with) the omitted سیمین. که + آید به کار is a relative clause: آید (3rd-person present) + به کار آمدن is an idiom meaning 'to be useful / to be needed'. So the clause means 'that are of use / that are needed.'
segments.s711-g52: Another genitive/list phrase introduced by ز: 'of dinars and of jewels...'. وز here is an archaic/coalesced form in the manuscript-level diction (functionally connecting the items, like 'and'), and گوهر شاهوار is a noun followed by an attributive adjective ('regal/lofty gem'). The adjective شاهوار follows the noun it modifies in this poetic order.
segments.s711-g53: فرستاد is the past tense 'sent' (simple past/dispatched). خاقان is the subject. به نزدیک + اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence/near him' — اوی is an enclitic pronoun meaning 'him'/'to him' (here marking the destination). The verb's object (who was sent) is not stated and is implied.
segments.s711-g54: درخشنده شد is a two-part predication: درخشنده (radiant/shining) + شد (became) = 'became radiant.' جان تاریک اوی is a noun phrase 'his dark soul/life' where جان ('soul, life') is modified by the adjective تاریک ('dark') and completed by the possessive/enclitic اوی ('his'). Note the adjective درخشنده precedes شد to form the copular/transformational sense.
segments.s711-g55: A sequence of destinations introduced by repeated به ('to'): به چوگان و مجلس به دشت شکار = 'to (the) polo and (the) court, to the hunting plain.' چوگان is the game (polo), مجلس here denotes the court/assembly, and دشت شکار is 'the hunting plain.' The repetition of به is a poetic coordination marking separate locales.
segments.s711-g56: نرفتی is the past-negative form 'you did not go' (classical past 2nd-singular with negation). مگر introduces a contrary/expecting particle (here 'unless' / 'then' in a rhetorical question), and کو is an interrogative/adverb 'where, where is.' بدی in the printed lemma list does not match the surrounding syntax: in context the word functions as a verb (archaic or contracted) rather than the listed noun 'evil', so no sense from the provided list applies here (-1). غمگسار is a noun meaning 'consoler/one who shares sorrow.' The whole clause is a rhetorical question: 'You did not go... so where were you a consoler?'
segments.s711-g97: بدو (to him) is an indirect-object prepositional adverb placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' and the sequence بدو گفت بهرام is a poetic/inverted order meaning 'Bahram said to him'. که introduces a subordinate (reported-speech) clause; اکنون پگاه = 'now at dawn' (اکنون = now, پگاه = dawn/early morning).
segments.s711-g98: چو آید is a temporal clause: چو (when) + آید (comes, 3rd-person present/future) 'when ... comes'. مقاتوره here names the person/party who will act; دینار خواه is a verb-like phrase meaning 'will want/claim dinars' — خواه functions as the future/volitional element. Word order places the time clause before the main content.
segments.s711-g99: This is a string of negative imperatives. مخند is an imperative negative (do not laugh/smile); the following و بر uses بر as a directional preposition/adverb 'toward' (the object/person is implied); هیچ مگشای چشم is a reinforced negative: هیچ 'at all' + مگشای (negative imperative 'do not open') + چشم 'eye'. The verbs are concise imperatives as typical in poetic counsel.
segments.s711-g100: مده پاسخ is another negative imperative construction: مده (do not give) + پاسخ (an answer). و‌گر introduces a conditional 'but if'; دهی is present/subjunctive 'you give'; جز به خشم means 'except in/with anger' (جز = except, به خشم = in anger). Together: 'Give no answer; but if you give one, let it be only in anger.'
segments.s711-g101: گذشت is a past-tense verb 'passed' with آن شب (that night) as its subject/complement: 'that night passed'. و links to the following temporal phrase; بامداد پگاه is a doubled poetic expression for morning/dawn (بامداد = morning, پگاه = dawn).
segments.s711-g102: بیامد (came) is the past tense 3rd-person verb; مقاتوره is the subject (the person/party who came). نزدیک شاه is a locative/adverbial phrase 'near the king' (نزدیک = near, شاه = king), so the clause means 'Maqatūreh came near the king.'
segments.s711-g103: جهاندار خاقان is a double title 'world-ruler, the Khaghan' (compound honorific subject). بدو is the indirect object 'at him/to him', and ننگرید (from نگریستن) is the past tense 'looked'/'regarded' — so: 'The sovereign Khaghan looked at him.' The verb follows the titles in normal poetic word order.
segments.s711-g104: نه negates the following clause: 'did not ...'. گفتار آن ترک جنگی is a noun phrase 'the speech/words of that Turk, a warlike one' (گفتار = words/speech; آن ترک جنگی = that warlike Turk; note the adjective جنگی follows ترک). شنید is the past-tense verb 'heard'. Together: 'and he did not hear the words of that warlike Turk.'
segments.s711-g121: نیرزد is an impersonal predicate 'it is not worth / it is not fitting' and is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. هر بامداد پگاه is an emphatic double adverbial of time ('every dawn / each morning at daybreak'), placed after که to specify when the action in the subordinate clause would take place.
segments.s711-g122: به خروار دینار is the object/measure phrase ('a kharvar/heap of dinars'); به here works adverbially/measure-wise ('by / in the amount of'). خواهی is the 2nd-person present/future-like verb 'you want / you will ask' and takes that measure as its object; ز شاه is a short, poetic prepositional phrase 'from the king' marking the source.
segments.s711-g123: This clause is Subject + Verb + Object: مقاتوره (here the acting subject/name or noun) + بشنید (past 'heard') + گفتار اوی ('his speech' where اوی is the enclitic genitive 'his'). The object گفتار takes اوی as possessive, a common older/poetic genitive form.
segments.s711-g124: سرش is 'his head' (noun + enclitic possessive ش). گشت is the past/copulative 'became / turned (into)'. پرکین functions as the predicative complement (here a name/epithet). ز آزار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of his torment' (ز = from, آزار = hurt/torment, اوی = his).
segments.s711-g125: بخشم here appears as a manner/stance ('in wrath' — an older/poetic form related to خشم) followed by و linking the next adverbial. به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with haste / swiftly' (به + تندی). بیازید is a sudden/action verb 'burst forth / sprang' (archaic/poetic form) and چنگ is its direct object (here 'claw' or metaphorical hand/strike).
segments.s711-g126: ز introduces source 'from'. ترکش here is written as ترک + ش (root + possessive enclitic) and functions as 'his quiver' / 'his case (for arrows)' in the clause. برآورد is the past/action verb 'drew forth / produced' (literally 'brought up'); تیر is the object 'an arrow' and خدنگ is a qualifier 'dagger-like / pointed', forming 'a dagger-arrow' or 'a sharp arrow'.
segments.s711-g127: به بهرام گفت is a simple indirect speech introduction 'he said to Bahram' (به marks the indirect object). این نشان is the demonstrative subject 'this token/sign'; منست is an older/poetic predicative form (here functioning as 'is mine / belongs to me' or 'is fittingly mine'), completing the reported clause.
segments.s711-g128: برزم is a possessive/locative phrase here ('in/at my barz/host/battle' — an older poetic form where م marks possession or relation to battle/host), اندرون is 'within', ترجمان is a predicate noun 'interpreter / representative', and منست again closes the clause as a predicative 'is (mine/appropriate)'. Together: 'It is the interpreter within my host.'
segments.s711-g105: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خاقان is the title 'Khaghan' (the ruler) and مقاتوره is a noun 'mqatūra' meaning 'battle/strife'. آمد is the past of 'to come' and links the whole noun phrase 'ز خاقان مقاتوره' as the source of what follows. بخشم here is best read as a contracted/poetic form of به خشم ‘into anger’ (i.e. ‘anger arose’), placed immediately after آمد to indicate the effect produced by the battle-noise.
segments.s711-g106: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one/every one'. برآشفت is a past/preterite form meaning 'became angry/rose in fury' (بر‑ prefix intensifies). و is the conjunction 'and'. بگشاد is a verbal or participial form from گشاد meaning 'opened' (here 'opened wide'), and چشم is the noun 'eye' — together 'opened their eyes'.
segments.s711-g107: بخاقان should be read as بِ خاقان 'to the Khaghan' (prepositional phrase marking the addressee); the printed lemma list does not supply a matching sense, so this is marked as not represented. چین here functions as a proper name or ethnonym 'the Chin/Chinese' and is the speaker. گفت is the verb 'said' introducing direct speech (the given senses list did not include the verbal sense, so it is marked accordingly). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned'.
segments.s711-g108: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. گشتم is first-person past of گشتن 'I became / I turned into' (here 'I have become/have been made'). امروز is the temporal adverb 'today'. پیش is 'in the presence of / before'. تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. خوار is an adjective 'despised/humiliated' and is the predicate complement of گشتم: 'why have I become humiliated before you today?'.
segments.s711-g109: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'. که is the relative/complementizer 'who/that' introducing the clause that follows. این is the demonstrative 'this'. مهتر is a title 'chief, leader'. پارسی is an adjective/noun 'Persian' describing مهتر. Together the phrase introduces 'this Persian chief' with a linking relative.
segments.s711-g110: The second که is again a relative 'who/that'. آمد is the past 'came'. The token ب is the preposition 'to/into' (here part of بدین = 'to this'); دین in the word list appears separated but here is part of the deictic 'بدین' ('to this'). مرز is 'frontier/border'. با is the preposition 'with'. یار is 'companion/ally'. Grammatically this clause says 'who came to this border with a companion.'
segments.s711-g111: بکوشد is a present/future-tense form of کوششیدن 'he will strive/endeavour'. همی is an adverb adding continuity/emphasis 'continually, still'. تا here introduces purpose/result 'so that/in order that'. بپیچی is a subjunctive/imperative form of پیچیدن 'you/one may turn' or 'he may make (turn)' depending on syntax; here it means 'to turn (away)'. ز is the preposition 'from'. داد is the noun 'justice' — 'so that (he) may make (you) turn away from justice.'
segments.s711-g112: سپاه is 'army, host'. تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'your/you' (here possessor). را is the definite object marker attached after the pronoun phrase. داد is again 'justice'. خواهد is the auxiliary 'will' (future), and بباد is a verbal phrase 'be blown away / be lost' — together 'your army's justice will be blown away' (i.e. justice will vanish from your host).
segments.s711-g65: بران سان (literally 'upon that + like') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that manner' or 'thus'. که introduces the subordinate/result clause. کهتر is an adjective 'lesser/ inferior' and کند is the simple present/past-tense-form (here classical narrational use) meaning 'makes' or 'causes'; آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' and functions as the direct object of کند. Word order is somewhat flexible and poetic: the adverbial phrase + conjunction starts the clause, then adjective + verb + object.
segments.s711-g66: This is a nominal phrase identifying the person whose praise was lessened: بران is a demonstrative 'that' (pointing back to someone just mentioned); نامبردار is an adjective 'renowned'; سالار is the head noun 'commander/chief' and چین is a genitive modifier 'of China'. The whole unit reads 'that renowned chief of China'.
segments.s711-g67: هم آنگه is a temporal/adverbial cluster: هم 'also/likewise' + آنگه 'then'. زدینار is the coin unit 'dinar' (here part of the quantity expression) and هزار is the numeral 'a thousand' which follows and is its quantifier. بردی is the past action 'took/removed' (classical poetic past form). The clause thus means 'then also he took a thousand dinars.'
segments.s711-g68: ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic form). گنج is the head noun 'treasure' and the following phrase جهاندیده نامدار are postpositive modifiers: جهاندیده 'world‑experienced/worldly‑wise' and نامدار 'renowned/illustrious' describe the owner or character of the treasure. Together this yields 'from the treasure of the renowned, worldly-wise (man)'.
segments.s711-g69: همی‌دید is an imperfective/formal past used in epic poetry to signal a continuous or repeated past action ('kept seeing' / 'kept watching'). The proper name بهرام follows (inverted order: verb before subject, common in poetry). یک چندگاه is an adverbial phrase 'for a while', indicating duration.
segments.s711-g70: به خاقان is a prepositional phrase marking the target of the gaze ('at/to the khagan'). همی‌کرد is an auxiliary-like imperfective 'kept doing' used here with the noun phrase خیره نگاه ('a fixed/staring gaze') to form a periphrastic expression meaning 'kept staring'. Note the object (خیره نگاه) follows the auxiliary, an order frequent in classical poetic usage.
segments.s711-g71: بخندید is the past tense 'he laughed' (classical/poetic past). یک روز is a temporal phrase 'one day'. گفت is the past tense 'he said' introducing direct speech (the lexical senses options are limited in the supplied list but here it functions as 'said'). ای بلند is a vocative: ای 'O!' followed by بلند 'lofty/loftily' used to address someone respectfully.
segments.s711-g72: توی here functions vocatively/identifyingly as 'you' (understood as 'thou/you' or 'you are') and is followed by a prepositional/relational phrase بر مهان جهان ارجمند meaning 'among the world's honored guests'. بر marks the relation to the group (poetic usage), مهان 'guests' is the group noun, جهان 'world' qualifies the group, and ارجمند 'esteemed/precious' is an epithet. Together: 'you, O noble one, among the world's honored guests.'
segments.s711-g113: بدو + گفت + بهرام is a compact classical order: the prepositional phrase 'بدو' (to him/unto him) comes before the verb 'گفت' (said) and the speaker 'بهرام'. 'که‌ای' is the vocative particle 'که ای' (O...), here addressing the addressee as 'جنگوی' (warrior).
segments.s711-g114: This clause contains the interrogative 'چرا' (why) followed by the perfective/preterite form 'تیزگشتی' (you became/are quick/eager) and the prepositional phrase 'بدین گفت‌وگو' (into/for this conversation). In classical word order the preposition+demonstrative ('به'+'این' written here as 'ب' + the following token) follows the verb cluster; 'گفت‌وگو' is the noun 'conversation'.
segments.s711-g115: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'خاقان' is the subject 'the khagan'. 'برد' here is a past/perfective form of بردن 'to take/leave' (he departed/took the road), followed by coordinated nouns 'راه و فرمان من' (the road and my command). Classical poetry often places verb and objects in flexible order.
segments.s711-g116: 'خرد را' (wisdom + object marker 'را') is topical/definite; 'نپیچد' = ن + پیچد is a negative present/subjunctive/jussive meaning 'does not/will not turn aside'. 'ز' (poetic for 'از') introduces origin 'from my پیمان' (covenant). Together: 'wisdom does not turn aside from my covenant.'
segments.s711-g117: 'نمانم' is first‑person negative form of ماندن (I will not remain / I shall not stay). The conjunction 'که' introduces the subordinate clause 'آیی تو هر بامداد' (that/so that you come every morning), with 'آیی' second‑person present/subjunctive 'you come' and 'هر بامداد' 'each dawn'.
segments.s711-g118: 'تن' (body) with the adverb/adjective 'آسان' (easily) and the verb 'دهی' (you give) forms 'you easily give/dispatch the body'; the phrase 'گنج او را به باد دادن' is an idiom 'to squander his treasure to the wind'. Word order puts the object 'گنج او را' before the prepositional idiom 'به باد'.
segments.s711-g119: 'بران نه' is a negative/discouraging phrase meaning 'it does not help / it is not for that'. The subordinate 'که' introduces the clause describing what does not help: 'هستی تو سیصد سوار' (that you are three hundred horsemen). Note the inverted/poetic order (normally 'تو سیصد سوار هستی').
segments.s711-g120: 'به رزم اندرون' is a prepositional phrase 'into/for battle' ('اندرون' = within, here 'in the field'), and 'شیرجویی شکار' is a noun‑phrase where 'شیرجویی' (lion‑hunting / daring exploit) modifies 'شکار' (prey/quarry). Together: 'in battle you go seeking lion‑hunting prey.'
segments.s711-g57: برین گونه is a fixed poetic phrase meaning “in this manner/thus” (بر + ین where ین is the orthographic/poetic form of این). بر بود is an older word order where the preposition/particle بر precedes بود (past copula) and links to the following noun: literally “on/thus was the Khaghan of China.” The phrase therefore states the existence/state of the Khaghan in that way.
segments.s711-g58: همی‌خواند is the older/heavy-imperfective/habitual form (همی‑ + خواند) meaning “kept singing/uttering.” It governs the object phrase بهرام را (بهرام marked by را as definite/accusative), and آفرین is the exclamation/word he utters; the verb introduces the continuous action of praising Bahram.
segments.s711-g59: یکی here is the indefinite/partitive “a certain.” نامبردار in older usage can mean “the one mentioned/renowned” or “the named/aforementioned,” and it modifies بد یار = “bad/evil companion.” اوی is an emphatic/old third‑person pronoun meaning “he.” Together: “a certain named/notorious bad companion of his.”
segments.s711-g60: اندرون is the locative “inside/inner quarters.” دست‌بردار here is a compound that in context functions as a substantive describing someone who lays hands (i.e. meddles or takes things) — an archaic or figurative sense; the line says this person operated inside the inner quarters. The first word برزم is archaic/poetic and unclear in isolation (see note).
segments.s711-g61: ازو is the ablative/agent “from him.” مه is used poetically (literally “moon”) as a metaphorical/epithetic term modifying گوهر (i.e. ‘moon-like’ or ‘radiant’), and به here links that epithet to گوهر (a stylistic ‘to/as a gem’). گوهر مقاتوره is a genitive/attributive sequence “the gem (or jewel) of combat/battle,” and نام finalizes the phrase as “the name/renown.” The whole fragment means roughly “from him, the battle‑gem’s name” (i.e. his martial fame).
segments.s711-g62: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause “for/that.” خاقان is the subject; ازو is again “from him”; یافتی is the past form of یافتن meaning “obtained/received” (third‑person past); and نام و کام are the two objects “name (fame) and kom (fulfillment/reward).” Grammatically: “that the Khaghan obtained from him name and fulfillment.”
segments.s711-g63: به + شبگیر is an adjunct meaning “towards/by/at the night‑visitor” or more simply “at night / to the night‑visitor”; نزدیک is the adjective/adverb “near,” and خاقان is the subject. شدی is an older past/auxiliary form meaning “became/was/moved” (here past action). Word order is topical/adverbial first (to‑night‑visitor), then predicate: literally “to the night‑visitor near the Khaghan became” → “the Khaghan approached the night‑visitor.”
segments.s711-g64: دولب را is the direct object marked by را. به انگشت خود is an instrumental/associative phrase “with/ by his finger” (به + noun). بر + زدی is a split verbal phrase: بر (on/upon) + زدی (past of زدن, “struck/hit”) — together “struck upon/ hit.” The clause therefore says he struck the doleb with his finger.
segments.s711-g129: چو + subjunctive: چو (when) with the verb بیایی (2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you come') yields a temporal clause 'when you come'. بدین is the preposition ب- + demonstrative دین here meaning 'to this' (to/into this), and بارگاه is the noun 'court/palace' (goal of the motion). Word order is typical Classical Persian: temporal conjunction + subjunctive verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s711-g130: همی‌دار is an archaic/poetic verbal form here meaning an imperative or hortative 'keep/hold' (literally 'continue to hold'); it governs the noun phrase پیکان ما را. پیکان ما را is 'our arrow' with را as the definite/object marker attached to the whole phrase. نگاه here functions as a noun 'watch/sight' (object of دار), so the whole clause means 'keep in sight/guard'.
segments.s711-g131: چو + past verb بشنید gives 'when he heard'. بهرام is the subject. شد is the past of شدن 'became' and takes the predicate تیز چنگ: تیز (an adjectival modifier 'sharp/quick') precedes the noun چنگ ('claw' literally, used figuratively for 'grasp/hand/pinch'). The phrase therefore expresses 'he became quick-fingered/quick of hand'.
segments.s711-g132: This is a compact noun phrase listing the weapon: یکی (numeral 'one') + تیر ('arrow/shaft'). پولاد ('steel') is an attributive modifier qualifying تیر; پیکان is a synonymous or amplifying noun for 'arrow/dart', and خدنگ is an appositive adjective/noun meaning 'dagger-like (point)/short-sword-like' describing the head. Classical Persian often strings modifiers and appositives without linking particles.
segments.s711-g133: بدو is the prepositional/dative phrase 'to him' (short form), placed before the verb in the classical word order. داد is the past tense 'gave'. و connects the clauses; گفتا is the narrative past 'said'. که introduces reported speech or content clause; این یادگار is the direct object of the forthcoming imperatives ('this memento').
segments.s711-g134: بدار is an imperative form 'keep' (from داشتن) addressing the addressee; و 'and' links the second imperative ببین ('see' or 'observe'). تا کی آید به کار is a temporal/result clause: تا 'until' (or 'to see how long'), کی 'when' (here 'how long / when'), آید subjunctive/present 'it will come' or 'it will prove', and به کار 'to use' meaning 'into use'—so 'see until/how long it will be useful.'
segments.s711-g135: مقاتوره is a nominalized form referring to 'the fighting/contest'. از پیش (from + before/in front of) with خاقان ('Khaghan, the ruler') indicates location 'from before the Khaghan' (i.e. away from his presence). برفت is a past tense 'went away/withdrew' (classical past of رفتن). Note: the supplied lemma for برفت in the metadata is the word 'برف' (snow), which is unrelated; here برفت should be read as 'he/it went away'.
segments.s711-g136: بیامد is a past/formal past 'came' or 'moved'; سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. خرگه خویش is a possessive phrase: خرگه 'lair/tent/stable' + خویش 'own' (here split as خوی + ش); خوی is the base and ش is the 3rd-person possessive clitic 'his'. تفت in the manuscript here is ambiguous (it can be a verb meaning 'to scorch' in the lexicon given) but in context is likely a proper name/place; the lexical senses provided do not fit straightforwardly, so that item is uncertain.

sentences.s711-s1: Now tell the ancient tales; speak the words of Bahram Chubin.
sentences.s711-s2: When he reached the Turks' city, he came into the presence of the brave and the great.
sentences.s711-s3: From the troop of wakeful-hearted men, ten thousand — selected horsemen — received him.
sentences.s711-s4: The son, with his brother, went before the inner court; and with each one a mobad as guide.
sentences.s711-s17: The khagan said to him, “O noble one, may you never be in need on this day.”
sentences.s711-s18: I will have you as a bond of my own; what bond is higher than that of one's own child?
sentences.s711-s19: All the people of the land stand with me as such allies, whether they are lesser or greater.
sentences.s711-s20: I will give you a place of honor among the chiefs, and I will grant you freedom from need even among the great.
sentences.s711-s9: When Bahram sat upon the silver throne, at that moment he took the khagan's hand in his own.
sentences.s711-s10: He said to him, 'O praiseworthy chief, commander of the Turks and lord of China.'
sentences.s711-s11: You know that from the sovereign of the world no one is safe in hiding.
sentences.s711-s12: He rests upon treasure and chooses it; he makes the body comfortable and increases its suffering.
sentences.s711-s5: When he came up onto the khaghan's throne, he praised him and led him to prayer.
sentences.s711-s6: When the khaghan saw him, he sprang to his feet, kissed him, and covered his face with his hand.
sentences.s711-s7: He questioned him at length about the hardships of the road, and about the king's affairs and the battles and the army.
sentences.s711-s8: He also questioned Izad, Goshasp and the brave-hearted, and asked Khirad and Barzine.
sentences.s711-s37: At each dawn, at the appointed time, such a man will receive a thousand dinars.
sentences.s711-s38: His gift was twenty tearful ones; all the gold‑mines were for him.
sentences.s711-s39: The khagan said to him that 'such is our custom and the radiance of our faith.'
sentences.s711-s40: For among us, whoever is the more warlike will be the more prone to hesitate in the hour of hardship.
sentences.s711-s13: If now you will receive me here, help me in good and in ill.
sentences.s711-s14: On this border where you are friendless, I am your companion; in good and bad I am your consoling friend.
sentences.s711-s15: And if any hardship befalls you, I will let it pass / bear it; I will entrust the whole land.
sentences.s711-s16: If now you are of the same mind, I will depart from here as far as India.
sentences.s711-s45: When the sovereign is awake, one must not entrust the reins (i.e. control) to an inferior.
sentences.s711-s46: If I free him (from it) for you, it may be possible for you; but if not, his particular favour (or special thing) will be required for you.
sentences.s711-s47: The khagan said to him, 'Your command is right; by this wish your counsel and pledge are right.'
sentences.s711-s48: If you could have freed him from me, you would have brought an end to all talk (all dispute).
sentences.s711-s21: At this Bahram also swore: he asked that harm be bound upon his life.
sentences.s711-s22: The khagan said to him, 'O Most High God, he is a guide for me and for you.'
sentences.s711-s23: For as long as I live I will be your special friend; in good and bad I will be your consoler.
sentences.s711-s24: After that they adorned two halls; they requested garments of every kind.
sentences.s711-s41: If he wants more, we have none left; there is no war‑maker among the valiant men.
sentences.s711-s42: His surplus is rightfully ours now; for a dinar we will recite an enchantment upon him.
sentences.s711-s43: When I take it back from him the troops will boil; because of the host a bright day will turn black.
sentences.s711-s44: The world‑seeker said, 'O head of the assembly, you have made him stare in amazement at you.'
sentences.s711-s25: Servants, coverings, and food — anything that ought to be laid out.
sentences.s711-s26: Of silver and gold that serve (are needed); of dinars and of regal gems.
sentences.s711-s27: The khaghan sent (men) to his presence; his dark soul became radiant.
sentences.s711-s28: You did not go to polo or the court or to the hunting plain — so where, then, were you a consoler of grief?
sentences.s711-s49: Bahram said to him that now, when dawn comes, Maqatūreh will want dinars.
sentences.s711-s50: Do not smile, nor turn (your face) toward him, and do not open your eyes at all; give no answer, and if you do give one, let it be only in anger.
sentences.s711-s51: That night passed, and at dawn the Maqatūreh came near the king.
sentences.s711-s52: The sovereign Khaghan looked at him, but he did not hear the words of that warlike Turk.
sentences.s711-s61: It is not worth that every dawn you should ask the king for a heap of dinars.
sentences.s711-s62: Maqatureh heard his speech; his head became Perkin from his torment.
sentences.s711-s63: In wrath, and with swiftness he burst forth (with) a claw; from his quiver he drew forth a dagger-like arrow.
sentences.s711-s64: He said to Bahram: 'This sign is mine; it is the interpreter within my host.'
sentences.s711-s53: From the Khaghan came the clash of battle and anger arose; one by one they grew enraged and opened their eyes.
sentences.s711-s54: To the Khaghan the Chin said, 'O renowned one, why have I become humiliated before you today?'
sentences.s711-s55: Truly, this Persian leader who came to this frontier with his companion (or companions)...
sentences.s711-s56: He will strive continually so that he may turn you away from justice; your army's justice will be blown away.
sentences.s711-s33: In that way he lessened the praise of that renowned lord, the chief of China.
sentences.s711-s34: Then likewise he took a thousand dinars from the treasure of the renowned, worldly-wise one.
sentences.s711-s35: For a while Bahram kept watching; he kept his gaze fixed upon the khagan.
sentences.s711-s36: One day he laughed and said, 'O lofty one, you among the honored guests of the world...'
sentences.s711-s57: Bahrām said to him, “O warrior, why do you rush into this conversation?”
sentences.s711-s58: When the khagan takes the road and my command, wisdom will not turn away from my covenant.
sentences.s711-s59: I will not remain so that you come every dawn and easily squander his treasure to the wind.
sentences.s711-s60: It avails not that you are three hundred horsemen — into battle you go seeking lion‑hunting prey.
sentences.s711-s29: Thus was the Khaghan of China; he kept crying “Afarin!” to Bahram.
sentences.s711-s30: A certain notorious ill-companion of his — he was a hand‑taker (a meddling/thieving man) in the inner quarters.
sentences.s711-s31: From him came the jewel-like renown of battle; for the Khaghan obtained from him both name (fame) and satisfaction.
sentences.s711-s32: At night the Khaghan drew near to the night‑visitor; he struck the doleb (knob/wheel) with his finger.
sentences.s711-s65: When you come to this court tomorrow, keep watch over our arrow.
sentences.s711-s66: When Bahram heard, he grew quick of hand; he seized a single steel arrow with a dagger-like head.
sentences.s711-s67: He gave it to him and said, 'Keep this memento and see how long it will be of use.'
sentences.s711-s68: The combat withdrew from before the Khaghan; it moved toward his own lair/tent (Taft).
